summary: Spolsky explores UI design principles centered on the difficulty users have controlling the mouse precisely. Building on Tog's 'mile high menu bar' concept, he argues that interfaces should accommodate imprecise mouse control rather than demanding pixel-perfect accuracy. He critiques specific UI elements like dropdown combo boxes, thin edit box fonts, and Windows' Start button placement for making mouse interaction unnecessarily difficult. The post introduces the programmer fallacy that 'all n's are equally likely' to explain why developers build interfaces requiring precision that users don't need. He advocates for designs like WinAmp's window snapping that intelligently compensate for imprecise mouse control, concluding that designers should treat users as if they have limited motor skills.
key_points: The 'mile high menu bar' principle shows that screen edges act as free precision aids — the Mac menu bar exploits this while Windows' Start button wastes it by sitting 2 pixels from the corner
Users struggle with mouse precision for many reasons: bad hardware, disabilities, inexperience, and the inherent slowness of multi-step mouse operations
Dropdown combo boxes are a UI sin — they should expand to show all items and allow clicking anywhere on the control, not just the tiny arrow
Edit boxes should use fat, monospace fonts because thin variable-width fonts make individual characters nearly impossible to select and distinguish
Programmers falsely assume 'all n values are equally likely,' leading them to allow pixel-perfect positioning when in reality certain positions (like screen edges) are overwhelmingly more useful
money_quotes: You should design your program so that it does not require a tremendous amount of mouse-agility to use it right.
For the sake of a couple of pixels, Microsoft literally 'snatches defeat from the jaws of victory,' Tog writes, and makes it that much harder to acquire the start button.
The poxy combo dropdown control is even more annoying because there's such an easy solution: just make the dropdown long enough to contain all of the options.
There are only three numbers: 0, 1, and n. If n is allowed, all n's are equally likely.

As it turns out, there are lots of good reasons why you might want a window exactly at the top of the screen, but there aren't any reasons to leave 2 pixels between the top of the screen and the top of the window.
Pretend that we are gorillas, or maybe smart orangutans, and we really have trouble with the mouse.
You lose a little bit of flexibility, but in exchange, you get a user interface that recognizes that controlling the mouse precisely is hard, so why should you have to?
The difference between a lower case I and a lower case L is literally one pixel.
key_insight: Good UI design assumes users have poor mouse control and compensates intelligently — through larger targets, screen-edge snapping, and fat fonts — rather than demanding pixel-perfect precision.
